title: Compressed Biogas (CBG) Initiatives
focus: Purification process of raw biogas and key Indian schemes promoting CBG.
rationale: A brief but distinct topic covering the technical definition of CBG and two major schemes (GOBAR-DHAN, SATAT) that are highly relevant for UPSC environment and energy questions.
sectionExcerpt: Raw biogas is purified to remove H2S, CO2, and water vapor, increasing methane concentration to 90%, and is then compressed to about 200-250 bar pressure to get CBG. Initiatives taken in India to promote CBG include the GOBAR-DHAN scheme to convert cattle dung and solid waste to Bio-CNG and compost, and the SATAT initiative for setting up CBG plants.
evidenceBullets: CBG production requires purifying raw biogas to 90% methane and compressing it to 200-250 bar pressure.
The GOBAR-DHAN scheme focuses on converting cattle dung and farm solid waste into Bio-CNG (CBG) and compost.
The SATAT initiative aims to set up Compressed Biogas (CBG) plants for affordable transportation.

title: Geothermal Energy and National Policy (NPGE, 2025)
focus: Geothermal energy basics, India's potential and provinces, and the National Policy on Geothermal Energy 2025.
rationale: A major topic with high examinability for UPSC, covering geographical locations (Puga, Chumathang, 10 provinces), technical details (enthalpy), and comprehensive policy frameworks.
sectionExcerpt: Geothermal energy harnesses Earth's crust heat. High-enthalpy (~200°C) resources suit electricity generation; low-medium (100-180°C) suit direct use. India's potential is 10,600 MW. GSI identified 381 hot springs and 10 provinces (Himalayan, Naga-Lushai, Andaman are orogenic). Puga and Chumathang in Ladakh are promising. NPGE 2025 (MNRE) aims to make geothermal a major renewable pillar for net-zero by 2070. It covers EGS, AGS, abandoned oil wells, and mineral by-products (silica, lithium). Financing includes RE-RTD (up to 100% for govt, 70% for industry). Recent steps include SCCL's 20 kW pilot in Telangana and Northeast's 1st well at Dirang, Arunachal Pradesh.
evidenceBullets: High-enthalpy geothermal resources (~200°C) are suitable for electricity generation, while low-to-medium (100-180°C) are for direct use.
India's estimated geothermal potential is 10,600 MW, with Puga and Chumathang in Ladakh being the most promising sites.
India has 10 Geothermal Provinces; Himalayan, Naga-Lushai, and Andaman Nicobar are orogenic, while the rest are non-orogenic.
NPGE 2025 allows extracting geothermal energy from abandoned oil/gas wells and recovering mineral by-products like lithium and cesium under the MMDR Act.
Exploration leases for geothermal can be granted for 3-5 years, and development leases for up to 30 years.
Northeast's first geothermal well is located at Dirang, Arunachal Pradesh, developed with Norway and Iceland.

title: Wind Energy and ALMM (Wind)
focus: Updates to the wind turbine approval system and India's wind energy sector statistics.
rationale: Covers the transition from RLMM to ALMM (Wind) and key statistics about India's wind energy capacity and potential, which are standard fodder for prelims statements.
sectionExcerpt: MNRE updated its wind turbine approval system, renaming RLMM to Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (Wind) or ALMM (Wind). It mandates sourcing key components from approved Indian suppliers and data localization. India ranks 4th globally in installed wind power capacity, contributing 4.69% to total electricity. Wind potential is 1164 GW at 150 meters above ground level.
evidenceBullets: MNRE updated the wind turbine approval system from RLMM to ALMM (Wind).
ALMM Wind mandates sourcing key components from approved Indian suppliers and requires data localization.
India ranks 4th in the world for installed wind power capacity.
India's wind potential is estimated at 1164 GW at 150 meters above ground level by the National Institute of Wind Energy.

title: Draft Guidelines for Underground Coal Gasification (UCG)
focus: Draft guidelines for UCG mining and mine closure plans.
rationale: A very brief section introducing the draft guidelines for UCG and the specific requirement of an escrow account with the CCO.
sectionExcerpt: Draft Guidelines for UCG deal with the preparation of mining and mine closure plans for UCG blocks. To ensure scientific mine closure, companies will be required to maintain an escrow account with the Coal Controller Organization (CCO).
evidenceBullets: Draft Guidelines for UCG require companies to maintain an escrow account with the Coal Controller Organization (CCO) to ensure scientific mine closure.
